lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Speak the speech, I pray you, as I pronounced it to you,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: trippingly on the tongue: but if you mouth it, as many of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: your players do, I had as lief the town-crier spoke my lines.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nor do not saw the air too much with your hand, thus,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: but use all gently; for in the very torrent, tempest, and, as

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I may say, the whirlwind of passion, you must acquire and beget

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a temperance that may give it smoothness. O, it offends me to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the soul to hear a robustious periwig-pated fellow tear a passion to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: tatters, to very rags, to split the ears of the groundlings, who

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: for the most part are capable of nothing but inexplicable dumbshows and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: noise: I would have such a fellow whipped for o’erdoing Termagant; it

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: out-herods Herod: pray you, avoid it.

lines.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
lines.text: I warrant your honour.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Be not too tame neither, but let your own discretion be your

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: tutor: suit the action to the word, the word to the action;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: with this special observance, that you o’erstep not the modesty of nature:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: for any thing so overdone is from the purpose of playing, whose

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: end, both at the first and now, was and is, to hold,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: as ’twere, the mirror up to nature; to show virtue her own

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: feature, scorn her own image, and the very age and body of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the time his form and pressure. Now this overdone, or come tardy

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: off, though it make the unskilful laugh, cannot but make the judicious

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: grieve; the censure of the which one must in your allowance o’erweigh

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a whole theatre of others. O, there be players that I have

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: seen play, and heard others praise, and that highly, not to speak

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: it profanely, that, neither having the accent of Christians nor the gait

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of Christian, pagan, nor man, have so strutted and bellowed that I

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: have thought some of nature’s journeymen had made men and not made

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: them well, they imitated humanity so abominably.

lines.speaker: FIRST PLAYER
lines.text: I hope we have reformed that indifferently with us, sir.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, reform it altogether. And let those that play your clowns speak

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: no more than is set down for them; for there be of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: them that will themselves laugh, to set on some quantity of barren

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: spectators to laugh too; though, in the mean time, some necessary question

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: of the play be then to be considered: that’s villainous, and shows

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a most pitiful ambition in the fool that uses it. Go, make

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you ready.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And the queen too, and that presently.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Bid the players make haste. Will you two help to hasten them?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: We will, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What ho! Horatio!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Here, sweet lord, at your service.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Horatio, thou art e’en as just a man

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As e’er my conversation coped withal.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: O, my dear lord—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nay, do not think I flatter;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For what advancement may I hope from thee

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That no revenue hast but thy good spirits,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To feed and clothe thee? Why should the poor be flatter’d?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, let the candied tongue lick absurd pomp,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And crook the pregnant hinges of the knee

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Where thrift may follow fawning. Dost thou hear?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Since my dear soul was mistress of her choice

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And could of men distinguish, her election

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hath seal’d thee for herself; for thou hast been

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As one, in suffering all, that suffers nothing,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A man that fortune’s buffets and rewards

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hast ta’en with equal thanks: and blest are those

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Whose blood and judgment are so well commingled,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That they are not a pipe for fortune’s finger

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To sound what stop she please. Give me that man

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That is not passion’s slave, and I will wear him

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In my heart’s core, ay, in my heart of heart,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As I do thee.—Something too much of this.—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: There is a play to-night before the king;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: One scene of it comes near the circumstance

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Which I have told thee of my father’s death:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I prithee, when thou seest that act afoot,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Even with the very comment of thy soul

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Observe mine uncle: if his occulted guilt

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Do not itself unkennel in one speech,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It is a damned ghost that we have seen,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And my imaginations are as foul

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As Vulcan’s stithy. Give him heedful note;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For I mine eyes will rivet to his face,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And after we will both our judgments join

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: In censure of his seeming.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Well, my lord:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: If he steal aught the whilst this play is playing,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And ’scape detecting, I will pay the theft.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: They are coming to the play; I must be idle:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Get you a place.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: How fares our cousin Hamlet?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Excellent, i’ faith; of the chameleon’s dish: I eat the air, promise-crammed:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you cannot feed capons so.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I have nothing with this answer, Hamlet; these words are not mine.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, nor mine now. To Polonius. My lord, you played once i’

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: the university, you say?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That did I, my lord; and was accounted a good actor.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What did you enact?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I did enact Julius Caesar: I was killed i’ the Capitol; Brutus killed me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It was a brute part of him to kill so capital a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: calf there. Be the players ready?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Ay, my lord; they stay upon your patience.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Come hither, my dear Hamlet, sit by me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, good mother, here’s metal more attractive.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: To the King. O, ho! do you mark that?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Lady, shall I lie in your lap? Lying down at Ophelia’s feet.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: No, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I mean, my head upon your lap?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Ay, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Do you think I meant country matters?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I think nothing, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That’s a fair thought to lie between maids’ legs.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: What is, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Nothing.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: You are merry, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Who, I?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Ay, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O God, your only jig-maker. What should a man do but be

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: merry? for, look you, how cheerfully my mother looks, and my father

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: died within these two hours.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Nay, ’tis twice two months, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So long? Nay then, let the devil wear black, for I’ll have

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: a suit of sables. O heavens! die two months ago, and not

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: forgotten yet? Then there’s hope a great man’s memory may outlive his

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: life half a year: but, by’r lady, he must build churches, then;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: or else shall he suffer not thinking on, with the hobby-horse, whose

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: epitaph is “For, O, for, O, the hobby-horse is forgot.”

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: What means this, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Marry, this is miching mallecho; it means mischief.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Belike this show imports the argument of the play.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: We shall know by this fellow: the players cannot keep counsel; they’ll tell all.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Will he tell us what this show meant?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, or any show that you’ll show him: be not you ashamed

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: to show, he’ll not shame to tell you what it means.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: You are naught, you are naught: I’ll mark the play.

lines.speaker: PROLOGUE
lines.text: For us, and for our tragedy,

lines.speaker: PROLOGUE
lines.text: Here stooping to your clemency,

lines.speaker: PROLOGUE
lines.text: We beg your hearing patiently.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Is this a prologue, or the posy of a ring?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: ’Tis brief, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As woman’s love.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Full thirty times hath Phoebus’ cart gone round

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Neptune’s salt wash and Tellus’ orbed ground,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: And thirty dozen moons with borrow’d sheen

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: About the world have times twelve thirties been

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Since love our hearts and Hymen did our hands

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Unite commutual in most sacred bands.

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: So many journeys may the sun and moon

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Make us again count o’er ere love be done!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: But, woe is me, you are so sick of late,

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: So far from cheer and from your former state,

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: That I distrust you. Yet, though I distrust,

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Discomfort you, my lord, it nothing must:

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: For women’s fear and love holds quantity;

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: In neither aught, or in extremity.

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Now, what my love is, proof hath made you know;

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: And as my love is sized, my fear is so:

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Where love is great, the littlest doubts are fear;

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Where little fears grow great, great love grows there.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: ’Faith, I must leave thee, love, and shortly too;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: My operant powers their functions leave to do:

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: And thou shalt live in this fair world behind,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Honour’d, beloved; and haply one as kind

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: For husband shalt thou—

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: O, confound the rest!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Such love must needs be treason in my breast:

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: In second husband let me be accurst!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: None wed the second but who kill’d the first.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Wormwood, wormwood.

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: The instances that second marriage move

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Are base respects of thrift, but none of love:

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: A second time I kill my husband dead,

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: When second husband kisses me in bed.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: I do believe you think what now you speak;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: But what we do determine oft we break.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Purpose is but the slave to memory,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Of violent birth, but poor validity:

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Which now, like fruit unripe, sticks on the tree;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: But fall, unshaken, when they mellow be.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Most necessary ’tis that we forget

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: To pay ourselves what to ourselves is debt:

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: What to ourselves in passion we propose,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: The passion ending, doth the purpose lose.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: The violence of either grief or joy

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Their own enactures with themselves destroy:

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Where joy most revels, grief doth most lament;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Grief joys, joy grieves, on slender accident.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: This world is not for aye, nor ’tis not strange

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: That even our loves should with our fortunes change;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: For ’tis a question left us yet to prove,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Whether love lead fortune, or else fortune love.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: The great man down, you mark his favourite flies;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: The poor advanced makes friends of enemies.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: And hitherto doth love on fortune tend;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: For who not needs shall never lack a friend,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: And who in want a hollow friend doth try,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Directly seasons him his enemy.

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: But, orderly to end where I begun,

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Our wills and fates do so contrary run

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: That our devices still are overthrown;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: Our thoughts are ours, their ends none of our own:

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: So think thou wilt no second husband wed;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: But die thy thoughts when thy first lord is dead.

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Nor earth to me give food, nor heaven light!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Sport and repose lock from me day and night!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: To desperation turn my trust and hope!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: An anchor’s cheer in prison be my scope!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Each opposite that blanks the face of joy

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Meet what I would have well and it destroy!

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Both here and hence pursue me lasting strife,

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: If, once a widow, ever I be wife!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If she should break it now!

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: ’Tis deeply sworn. Sweet, leave me here awhile;

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: My spirits grow dull, and fain I would beguile

lines.speaker: PLAYER KING
lines.text: The tedious day with sleep. Sleeps.

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: Sleep rock thy brain;

lines.speaker: PLAYER QUEEN
lines.text: And never come mischance between us twain!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Madam, how like you this play?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, but she’ll keep her word.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Have you heard the argument? Is there no offence in’t?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, no, they do but jest, poison in jest; no offence i’ the world.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: What do you call the play?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The Mouse-trap. Marry, how? Tropically. This play is the image of a

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: murder done in Vienna: Gonzago is the duke’s name; his wife, Baptista:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you shall see anon; ’tis a knavish piece of work: but what

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: o’ that? your majesty and we that have free souls, it touches

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: us not: let the galled jade wince, our withers are unwrung.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: You are as good as a chorus, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I could interpret between you and your love, if I could see the puppets dallying.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: You are keen, my lord, you are keen.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It would cost you a groaning to take off my edge.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Still better, and worse.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So you must take your husbands. Begin, murderer; pox, leave thy damnable

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: faces, and begin. Come: “the croaking raven doth bellow for revenge.”

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: Thoughts black, hands apt, drugs fit, and time agreeing;

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: Confederate season, else no creature seeing;

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: Thou mixture rank, of midnight weeds collected,

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: With Hecate’s ban thrice blasted, thrice infected,

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: Thy natural magic and dire property,

lines.speaker: LUCIANUS
lines.text: On wholesome life usurp immediately. Pours the poison into the sleeper’s ears.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He poisons him i’ the garden for’s estate. His name’s Gonzago: the

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: story is extant, and writ in choice Italian: you shall see anon

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: how the murderer gets the love of Gonzago’s wife.

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: The king rises.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What, frighted with false fire!

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: How fares my lord?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Give o’er the play.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Give me some light: away!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Lights, lights, lights!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, let the stricken deer go weep, The hart ungalled play; For

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: some must watch, while some must sleep: So runs the world away.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Would not this, sir, and a forest of feathers—if the rest of

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: my fortunes turn Turk with me—with two Provincial roses on my razed

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: shoes, get me a fellowship in a cry of players, sir?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Half a share.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A whole one, I. For thou dost know, O Damon dear, This

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: realm dismantled was Of Jove himself; and now reigns here A very,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: very—pajock.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: You might have rhymed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O good Horatio, I’ll take the ghost’s word for a thousand pound. Didst perceive?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Very well, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Upon the talk of the poisoning?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I did very well note him.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ah, ha! Come, some music! come, the recorders!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: For if the king like not the comedy,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why then, belike, he likes it not, perdy.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Come, some music!

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Good my lord, vouchsafe me a word with you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, a whole history.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: The king, sir—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, sir, what of him?

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Is in his retirement marvellous distempered.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With drink, sir?

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: No, my lord, rather with choler.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Your wisdom should show itself more richer to signify this to his

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: doctor; for, for me to put him to his purgation would perhaps

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: plunge him into far more choler.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Good my lord, put your discourse into some frame and start not

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: so wildly from my affair.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I am tame, sir: pronounce.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: The queen, your mother, in most great affliction of spirit, hath sent me to you.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You are welcome.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Nay, good my lord, this courtesy is not of the right breed.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: If it shall please you to make me a wholesome answer, I

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: will do your mother’s commandment: if not, your pardon and my return

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: shall be the end of my business.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, I cannot.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: What, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Make you a wholesome answer; my wit’s diseased: but, sir, such answer

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: as I can make, you shall command; or, rather, as you say,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: my mother: therefore no more, but to the matter: my mother, you

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: say—

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Then thus she says; your behavior hath struck her into amazement and admiration.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O wonderful son, that can so astonish a mother! But is there

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: no sequel at the heels of this mother’s admiration? Impart.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: She desires to speak with you in her closet, ere you go to bed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: We shall obey, were she ten times our mother. Have you any

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: further trade with us?

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: My lord, you once did love me.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So I do still, by these pickers and stealers.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Good my lord, what is your cause of distemper? you do, surely,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: bar the door upon your own liberty, if you deny your griefs

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: to your friend.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sir, I lack advancement.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: How can that be, when you have the voice of the king

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: himself for your succession in Denmark?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, but sir, “While the grass grows,”—the proverb is something musty.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: O, my lord, if my duty be too bold, my love is too unmannerly.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I do not well understand that. Will you play upon this pipe?

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: My lord, I cannot.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I pray you.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Believe me, I cannot.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I do beseech you.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: I know no touch of it, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis as easy as lying: govern these ventages with your fingers and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: thumb, give it breath with your mouth, and it will discourse most

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: eloquent music. Look you, these are the stops.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: But these cannot I command to any utterance of harmony; I have not the skill.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, look you now, how unworthy a thing you make of me!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: You would play upon me; you would seem to know my stops;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you would pluck out the heart of my mystery; you would sound

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: me from my lowest note to the top of my compass: and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: there is much music, excellent voice, in this little organ; yet cannot

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: you make it speak. ’Sblood, do you think I am easier to

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: be played on than a pipe? Call me what instrument you will,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: though you can fret me, yet you cannot play upon me.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: My lord, the queen would speak with you, and presently.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Do you see yonder cloud that’s almost in shape of a camel?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By the mass, and ’tis like a camel, indeed.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Methinks it is like a weasel.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: It is backed like a weasel.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or like a whale?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Very like a whale.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then I will come to my mother by and by. They fool

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: me to the top of my bent. I will come by and

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: by.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I will say so.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: By and by is easily said. Leave me, friends.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Tis now the very witching time of night,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: When churchyards yawn and hell itself breathes out

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Contagion to this world: now could I drink hot blood,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And do such bitter business as the day

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Would quake to look on. Soft! now to my mother.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O heart, lose not thy nature; let not ever

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The soul of Nero enter this firm bosom:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let me be cruel, not unnatural:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I will speak daggers to her, but use none;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My tongue and soul in this be hypocrites;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How in my words soever she be shent,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To give them seals never, my soul, consent!

stage_directions.text: How now, my lord! I will the king hear this piece of work?
Danish march. A flourish. Enter King, Queen, Polonius, Ophelia, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, and others.

Enter a King and a Queen very lovingly; the Queen embracing him, and he her. She kneels, and makes show of protestation unto him. He takes her up, and declines his head upon her neck: lays him down upon a bank of flowers: she, seeing him asleep, leaves him. Anon comes in a fellow, takes off his crown, kisses it, and pours poison in the King’s ears, and exit. The Queen returns; finds the King dead, and makes passionate action. The Poisoner, with some two or three Mutes, comes in again, seeming to lament with her. The dead body is carried away. The Poisoner wooes the Queen with gifts: she seems loath and unwilling awhile, but in the end accepts his love. Exeunt.
Enter two Players, King and Queen.
This is one Lucianus, nephew to the king.
Exeunt all but Hamlet and Horatio.
O, the recorders! let me see one. To withdraw with you:—why do you go about to recover the wind of me, as if you would drive me into a toil?

glosses.definition: cheapest theatergoers standing in the pit
loud ranting character from medieval drama
outdoes the ranting Herod (of mystery plays)
vice to see its own ugliness

annotations.title: Hamlet's lecture on acting — Shakespeare the director
annotations.body: Hamlet's advice to the players ('speak the speech trippingly on the tongue,' 'suit the action to the word, the word to the action') is the most sustained theory of acting in Elizabethan drama. It is also the closest Shakespeare ever comes to direct autobiography. The critic A. R. Humphreys argued that the speech reflects the working principles of the Lord Chamberlain's Men — restraint over noise, naturalism over ranting, the actor as the age's 'abstract and brief chronicle.' What Hamlet attacks — actors who 'saw the air,' 'out-herod Herod,' 'tear a passion to tatters' — is the older, bombastic style of Edward Alleyn and the Admiral's Men. Shakespeare is, through his prince, staking a claim for a new theatrical aesthetic.
sources: Andrew Gurr, The Shakespearean Stage 1574–1642 (3rd ed., 1992)

annotations.title: Hamlet's praise of Horatio and the stoic ideal
annotations.body: Hamlet's tribute to Horatio — 'Give me that man / That is not passion's slave' — articulates the stoic ideal the prince himself cannot achieve. The virtues Hamlet names (not being 'a pipe for fortune's finger,' being unmoved by both blessing and blow) are those of Seneca and Boethius, standard fare in the Wittenberg curriculum. The emotional force of the speech lies in its confession: Hamlet knows what he ought to be and is not. Horatio is the character the play preserves at its end, the one who will 'live behind to tell my story,' because his temperament is what the world of the play systematically destroys.
sources: Rhodri Lewis, Hamlet and the Vision of Darkness (2017)

annotations.title: 'The Mousetrap' and the logic of the play-within
annotations.body: The play Hamlet has commissioned — The Murder of Gonzago — re-enacts his father's murder: poison poured into an ear, a widow queen who remarries the murderer. Hamlet's ironic renaming ('the Mousetrap') is precise; the play is a trap, and the target is Claudius's conscience. The theory that drives the plot is one articulated by Puritan preachers and Shakespeare's own Henry V: theatrical representation has the power to prick the guilty. When Claudius rises at the poisoning scene and calls for light, the test has worked. What the test does not give Hamlet is a legally admissible confession or a path to action. The scene ends with him no closer to revenge than he began, only more certain.

annotations.title: 'Play upon this pipe' — the music of being read
annotations.body: Hamlet's image of the recorder is among the play's most precise metaphors for agency. 'It is as easy as lying,' he tells Guildenstern, who cannot play the pipe. 'Govern these ventages with your finger and thumb, give it breath with your mouth, and it will discourse most eloquent music.' The instrument, Hamlet means, is simpler than a man, and yet Guildenstern cannot make it speak; how then does he imagine he can play Hamlet? The scene ends with Hamlet's most direct accusation of his friends: 'You would seem to know my stops; you would pluck out the heart of my mystery.' The pipe image reveals the play's theory of selfhood — there is something in a man that resists being played.

trials.prompt: What style of acting does Hamlet criticize in his advice to the players?
options: The naturalistic whisper of boy actors in girls' roles.
The bombastic, air-slashing ranting of actors who 'out-herod Herod.'
The monotone declamation of foreign troupes.
The mumbling of country actors who cannot be heard in the galleries.

trials.prompt: What quality in Horatio does Hamlet admire most?
options: His physical courage in battle.
His calm imperviousness to fortune — he is 'not passion's slave.'
His knowledge of Latin and Greek.
His connections at the English court.

trials.prompt: Why does Hamlet arrange the performance of 'The Murder of Gonzago'?
options: To entertain the King at a festival.
To re-enact his father's murder and observe Claudius for signs of guilt.
To impress Ophelia with his poetic skill.
To raise money for the traveling players.

trials.prompt: What does Claudius's reaction to the play's poisoning scene confirm?
options: That the ghost was a malign spirit deceiving Hamlet.
That the ghost's account of the murder was accurate.
That Claudius is unable to tolerate violent drama.
That Gertrude was complicit in the murder.

trials.prompt: In the recorder scene, what is Hamlet's accusation against Rosencrantz and Guildenstern?
options: That they have poisoned the King's wine.
That they imagine they can play upon him though they cannot even play a simple pipe.
That they plan to betray him to the English king.
That they have stolen his signet ring.

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic function of the play-within-the-play?
options: To give the audience a break from Hamlet's soliloquies.
To convert a private testimony (the ghost's) into public theatrical evidence Claudius cannot evade.
To introduce the character of Lucianus, who returns in Act 5.
To allow Hamlet to audition for a career as a playwright.
